I've shown this picture of the ventral and the dorsal pathway, and the idea here is that rather than thinking about this, it used to be thought of as, well, this is about what's out there and this is about where it's at.
That's sort of true, this what versus where, but maybe the better way to think about it is that this is about what, like what's the shape, what's the color, what's the texture.

This ventral pathway is really important for recognizing objects, has strong links to memory because that is to basically recognize an object is a memory task, and this information notably is really accessible to consciousness, okay, what we can tell you about, like we're asking her to tell us what's the orientation you see, and she's basically giving us, you know, that she doesn't actually have that information.

Whereas this dorsal pathway, maybe instead of thinking about where, it's really about how, how to act in the world given the visual input, and that's really sensitive to spatial perception, the dynamics such as motion, but again, the purpose of vision here, maybe it's actually not so much to actually recognize things, but about allowing us to interact with the world, and again, what's really interesting to me here is that it seems like information in the dorsal pathway isn't really accessible to consciousness, right, and that seems just really counterintuitive.
Ask her what's the orientation, she can't tell you, have her pick up the card and put it in the slot, she does perfectly at it.
So she doesn't, she can't tell you what she's seeing, and yet her action system is able to take that information and act upon it.
All right, so those are sort of some dramatic case studies.

The general category or general syndrome when people have deficits in object recognition is what's called visual agnosia, and again, the word gnostic means knowledge, so you might think, oh, it's a loss of knowledge, but again, visual agnosia means it's a loss of knowledge through the visual pathways, so it's a deficit in recognizing a visually presented object, and it can't be attributed to some problem with some sort of sensory ability, so it isn't because they can't see, it's that the information's getting in, but they just can't organize it into a recognizable percept.

That seems to be individuals who actually can pass that test I just showed you about these two different views of the stapler, but what their problem really seems to be at a very late stage.
They can organize that visual information, but they can't make that connection to our stored knowledge, our memories of that information.
So they can't really access that semantic knowledge or that functional information, so if you gave them this test, you said, which of these three things go together best, right?

So these would be, again, if you're going by the physical similarity, you'd say these two things, but if you said, which of the two things that depicts a similar object, now your answer should be these two here, or if you got this test, your correct answer would be these, that these two are just basic chairs, this is a specialized chair.
And that's the kind of test that people with associative agnosia will have difficulty with.
They'll recognize that all three of these things are chair-like, but if you ask them, what about the function of them, then they have difficulty accessing that sort of information.

And that's what we'd call in neuropsychology a disconnection syndrome, meaning it's a disconnection between the visual processing and that sort of long-term knowledge, that somehow they can't really make that connection from the visual information to all our stored knowledge about what all these objects, what do we do with them, and so forth.
And what gets really bizarre with these associative agnosias is that some of them will show these really strange category-specific deficits.

The idea here is that when we represent living things, we almost always entirely think about visual objects, like the leopard is spotted, or like it's got a shape like a cat and so forth.

When we think about non-living things, okay, certainly we can describe their physical properties, you know that a stove might be, I don't know how you would describe it, white or something, but notice what we did is we talked more about the function of a stove, we talked about the stove, the stove as being a place for cooking and so forth.

And so the idea here is that if I'm trying to think about a bird, well, there's really only one way I'm going to access that knowledge, and that's through the visual analysis, through its recognition of all the visual properties that define a bird, that it has wings, that it has two legs, and so forth.

But actually, when I'm talking about, say, something like scissors, I have knowledge about the shape of it and so forth, you know, it's sharp and pointy and whatever, but I also have some knowledge, and that's sort of why I've highlighted the sensory motor cortex, about the kinds of actions that I might take with that object.
So the idea is that non-living things basically have multiple pathways to access our long-term knowledge about them.

Some of that's probably going to go through that ventral pathway, some of it can go across the dorsal pathway to kind of activate the knowledge we have about how we interact with those objects, but we don't have those sorts of representations associated with things like a leopard, you know.
None of you were writing about, you know, riding on top of leopards and so forth.

So that's sort of one account about why people have such more difficulty with living things compared to the non-living things, because with the living things, the representation is limited to a particular format.
Which brings us to perhaps the most interesting type of visual agnosia, which is prosopagnosia.
So this is another category-specific deficit, but here we're talking about a deficit just in particular in recognizing faces.

So again, it's restricted to the visual modality. You can read these case reports of people where they'll talk about, you know, after a stroke, I'd never longer recognize people, but they'll always say, as soon as the person spoke to me, like if it was my wife or something like that, as soon as she spoke to me, suddenly things were very clear.
And it's a really interesting phenomenon that they say, you know, I couldn't recognize this person.
I now hear their voice, and suddenly that blob in front of me takes shape and actually is something that's highly recognizable.
So once they've got the label, now they can recognize the person, but they have the striking inability to just recognize the person from their facial features.
And there's cases where people talk about that they don't recognize themselves when they look in the mirror.
Now, there's a distinction between acquired prosopagnosia. So acquired would mean that it's something you got late in life.
So these are individuals who used to be able to recognize faces, and suddenly they've had some sort of brain injury along that ventral pathway, and they've lost their ability to recognize faces.
There's also an interesting syndrome about developmental prosopagnosia. right? That's people who think that, I just don't recognize faces well.
And there's a debate about, you know, is there a similarity between these two disorders?
Are there similar brain structures and so forth? You can test yourself about developmental prosopagnosia.

So if you come up to the outside class, you got to introduce yourself each time.
But the question is that people asking the acquired prosopagnosia or studies there is, you know, what is it about this disorder?
Why are there so many individuals? Not so many. It's still a rare disorder.
But nonetheless, this category-specific agnosia of prosopagnosia. And one notion is that, well, you know, recognizing faces is really a hard task, right?
All faces sort of look alike. They got two eyes.
They got a nose. They got a mouth. You know, a forehead and everything.
And so maybe it's just a very difficult discrimination, especially, say, if you're comparing discriminating, you know, a roll of tape versus a pen.
That is that faces are kind of like a within-category discrimination, whereas most object tests are between.
And here's one of my favorite studies. Again, it's a British study.
I don't know any of these faces, and it's not because of my developmental prosopagnosia.

And they gave him three tests. They said, can they recognize faces?

63% sounds pretty good. W.J.'s actually doing better than the other cohort, the control cohort.

The control participants, great at faces, you know, we don't know if that's good or bad.

shows the opposite pattern of results. So it really argues against this idea that it's really about within category discriminations.
It seems to be something specific about faces that's a problem.
Moreover, something about his face perception problem may actually be benefiting his ability to recognize sheep.

So the other notion that people have really pursued is the idea that, well, maybe face perception is so important to our existence because we're such a social species that maybe we've really developed a specialized system within the brain that's almost dedicated to face perception.
And that's sort of like this notion about thinking about what's really important.
Those are the forces that are going to shape how brains get organized and develop.

That's led to what's been called the fusiform face area, is a particular region right on the bottom, so right on that ventral surface of the temporal lobe, and the fusiform face area is in various different imaging studies.
This is from an fMRI study, regions that show real selectivity to face stimuli compared to all other types of classes of objects.

And these are just one example of it. Here they're actually going to alternate between showing faces and objects, faces, objects, go back and forth, I think they were going about every 40 seconds or so, they'd flip from faces to then objects and so forth, always showing a unique stimulus each time.

And just looking at the bold response from this region right here, you can see sharp increase when you get the faces, a little bit of a response to the objects, sharp increase to faces, much smaller response to the objects and so forth.
